Here's the setup: → I wrote 10 specific AI prompts covering every role a brand needs (Growth, Ads, Email, CFO, Merchandiser, CRO). -> I connected them to live data (Shopify, Meta, Google, Klaviyo, etc.,) using Polar MCP. → I scheduled them all to run simultaneously using Claude Cowork. Every task is tagged with a priority level, and the exact numbers backing up the decision. The team just opens Notion and executes. Zero dashboards required. The winning stack isn’t more AI. It’s AI grounded in live data, shipped into workflows. I am giving away the exact blueprint so you can do this yourself. Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: Today we’re announcing that Opine has raised nearly $5M in funding, including $3M in fresh funding led by S3 Ventures!

Today we’re announcing that Opine has raised nearly $5M in funding, including $3M in fresh funding led by S3 Ventures! It's been a beast of a journey since Austin, Charlie and I incorporated Opine in 2023 with literally just a team and the vague idea of helping complex B2B tech vendors sell, grow, and adapt more efficiently. I never thought that mission would allow me to go all in on building for presales engineering, which was my previous leadership role at a unicorn startup. This round is really a bet on a group that doesn’t get nearly enough credit, presales and solutions engineering teams. They sit at the center of the most important conversations in complex B2B tech sales, discovery, evaluation, architecture, risk, yet they’re often under-resourced and under-instrumented compared to their impact. So many teams rely on presales and solutions intelligence whenever their work becomes business critical. Sales, Product, Marketing, the list goes on. We started Opine because we kept seeing the same pattern: brilliant presales teams held everything together with spreadsheets, threads, tribal knowledge, and heroic effort. When that invisible work isn’t captured, it hurts everyone. The business loses voice of the customer context, leadership risks balloon, post-sales inherits chaos, and customers feel the seams. Our goal is to turn that “undervalued” reality into a compounding advantage. By giving presales an AI-native system that understands their workflows, captures every evaluation and handoff, and surfaces deal risk in real time, we can make them the core of the entire GTM motion. When presales wins, sales, customer success, and product all win. I’m deeply grateful to our customers like Docker, Inc, Tailscale, Gainsight, BigID, Cyberhaven, Socket, Beyond Identity, ConductorOne, and many others who have trusted us early, and pushed us to build something worthy of their teams. Thank you as well to our partners at S3 Ventures, Knoll Ventures, Atlanta Seed Company, Gray Ventures, Propel Venture Partners, Triangle Tweener Fund, WndrCo, Diede van Lamoen and Feross Aboukhadijeh for believing in this vision. We’re just getting started. Presales deserves tools and recognition that match their importance. Our commitment with Opine is simple: make their work visible, make their impact undeniable, and quietly become the most valuable tool in the GTM stack just like they are quietly the most valuable department. (🎥 More on the story and what’s next in the video.)

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: I've spent at least 60' every day for the last couple of months vibe coding on Claude Code...

I've spent at least 60' every day for the last couple of months vibe coding on Claude Code... and most of the time I did it wrong! Defining the right anatomy of the .claude/ folder has defined a before and an after on the quality of my side projects. Most new users skip the setup. They open Claude Code and start prompting raw. No structure. No rules. No memory. That's the mistake I did. The .claude folder is Claude's operating system for your project. Get it right and Claude stops guessing. Get it wrong and you spend half your time correcting it. Here's the anatomy that works: 👉 CLAUDE.md → Claude's instruction manual. Build commands, architecture decisions, conventions, gotchas. Keep it under 200 lines. This is your highest-leverage file. 👉 rules/ → When CLAUDE.md gets crowded, split by concern. code-style.md, testing.md, api-conventions.md. Scope rules to specific paths with YAML frontmatter so they only load when relevant. 👉 commands/ → Repeatable workflows as slash commands. Code review, issue fixing, deploy checks. They run shell commands and inject real output into the prompt. 👉 skills/ → Like commands but Claude triggers them automatically when the task matches. They're packages, not single files. 👉 agents/ → Isolated subagent personas with their own tools and model preferences. A code reviewer that only reads. A security auditor scoped to grep. 👉 settings.json → Permission control. What Claude can run freely, what it must ask about, what's blocked entirely. The part most people miss: there are two .claude folders. One in your project (committed, shared with the team) and one at ~/.claude/ (personal, global across all repos). The .claude folder is infrastructure. Treat it like one. Full guide in the article below in the comments 👇 #ai #claude

Write a LinkedIn post for ai about workflow. Angle: You're using the wrong AI.

You're using the wrong AI. And it's costing you. Most businesses grab Generative AI for everything. Then wonder why results are underwhelming. The issue isn't AI. It's using the wrong AI for the job. Here's the framework: Machine Learning → Predict outcomes ↳ Use when you need forecasts, risk scores, or demand planning. Neural Networks → Recognize patterns ↳ Use when you need image analysis, voice processing, or recommendations. Generative AI → Create + synthesize ↳ Use when you need content, code, summaries, or complex problem-solving. AI Agents → Execute tasks ↳ Use when you need workflow automation or system integrations. Agentic AI → Run operations ↳ Use when you need autonomous decision-making at scale. But here's what most people miss: Start from the BOTTOM. Master each layer before climbing up. Most companies skip to GenAI because it's sexy. Then wonder why their AI "doesn't work." The reality? → No ML foundation = garbage predictions → No predictions = blind automation → Blind automation = expensive mistakes The companies winning with AI in 2026? They're not chasing trends. They're stacking capabilities. Layer by layer. Problem by problem. What's ONE problem you're trying to solve with AI right now? If this clarified your AI strategy: ♻️ Save this for your next AI decision 🔁 Repost to help your network avoid costly mistakes ➕ Follow Gabriel Millien for more actionable frameworks Infographic Credit: Aditya Sharma, give him a follow!

Prediction: Token budgets will soon surpass human salaries. We are entering an era where the cost of compute for reasoning is becoming a primary line item, and the numbers are scaling fast. I am already seeing developers consuming $100k+ a week in tokens. It sounds astronomical, but when you look at the output, the ROI is there. They aren't just running a single chat thread; they are spinning up massive parallel processes. Swarms of agents coding, debugging, testing, and architecting simultaneously. The velocity is incredible. But at that scale, cloud inference costs become the bottleneck. The big unlock? Local Compute. Putting a Mac Mini (or equivalent high-performance local hardware) on every developer's desk to run local models is the inevitable next step. We need to move the heavy lifting to the edge to make this sustainable. Invest $10,000 on a local computer and you have unlimited AI. This creates a massive new need for the Enterprise: Fleet Management for Models. Just as we manage laptops with MDM, companies will need a way to manage these distributed AI systems; securely pushing the latest Open Source models to local machines, updating weights, and orchestrating the workflow between local "thinking" and cloud "verification." The future is hybrid, and the infrastructure to support it is being built right now.

Write a LinkedIn post for data about workflow. Angle: An OpenAI employee asked ChatGPT to build a DCF.

An OpenAI employee asked ChatGPT to build a DCF. It valued Uber at $20 trillion. That's 2/3 of US GDP. The post went viral. Not because it was good. Because it was absurd. 130x Uber's actual market cap. From one lazy prompt. Here's what most people missed: The AI did exactly what it was told. The problem was the input. Not the tool. One vague sentence. No structure. No methodology. No guardrails. Garbage in, garbage out. That rule didn't change because the tool got smarter. So I built a 4-prompt DCF workflow in ChatGPT 5.2 that actually works. Here's the framework: Prompt 1: Build a blank DCF template → 9 tabs with Goldman-style formatting → WACC build, sensitivity tables, equity bridge → Mid-year convention. Blue font for inputs Prompt 2: Pull historical financials → 3-5 years of actuals from SEC filings → Income statement, balance sheet, cash flows → Separate chat to protect data fidelity Prompt 3: Research forward assumptions → Street consensus and management guidance → WACC from comps, revenue growth trajectory → Terminal value cross-checks built in Prompt 4: Build the model and QC it → Populates the template with assumptions → Sanity checks on terminal value and growth → Full sensitivity across WACC and TV One prompt gave someone Uber at $20 trillion. Four prompts gave me something I'd put in front of an IC. The difference? Structure. Most people try AI once. Get garbage output. Then decide the tool is useless. It's not. You just never learned how to use it. That's the real risk here. Not that AI replaces you. It's that you dismiss the most powerful tool of your generation because you fed it a bad prompt. Learn to prompt properly or keep thinking AI is a gimmick. Your call. --- 👉 P.S. I put together a full guide with all 4 prompts ready to copy-paste. Download here: https://lnkd.in/euxRknVs

I asked an AI vendor to explain what an agent is last week. The room went quiet. Their deck had "AI-powered" on every slide. Their demo looked great. Vendors are slapping "AI agent" on tools that are just workflows with a fresh coat of paint. And most buyers don't know enough yet to call it out. So I've started asking four questions in every pitch. They cut through the hype faster than any demo. "Explain what an agent is." If they can't articulate the difference between autonomous reasoning and a predetermined workflow, they're selling a label, not a capability. "What model powers this?" I've had vendors refuse to answer. Others mumble about "proprietary AI." If they built something real, they'd know exactly what's running under the hood. "Do you support open APIs or MCP?" Model Context Protocol lets AI tools connect to your existing systems and pull context from your work environment. If a vendor looks blank when you mention it, they haven't thought about how their tool fits into your tech stack. And an isolated AI tool loses most of its value. "How does token usage affect my pricing?" Hidden costs bury firms that skip this question. Get the math before you get the invoice. I keep this list short for a reason. You don't need twenty questions. You need four that the unprepared can't fake their way through. And here's what worries me most about bad vendor purchases: they don't just waste budget. They make the whole firm skeptical. The next time someone proposes a legitimate AI initiative, they're fighting the ghost of that failed tool. The vendors worth working with welcome these questions. They're proud of their answers. The ones who squirm? That tells you everything. What questions are you asking AI vendors?

Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: If your positioning strategy is “AI Agents for ______,” You’ll mainly sell to early adopters.

If your positioning strategy is “AI Agents for ______,” You’ll mainly sell to early adopters. Early adopters are technology-first, workflow-second. “Give me the tech, and I’ll find the best way to use it.” There’s nothing wrong with getting your product into the hands of early adopters. But let’s say you’ve raised your series A and your investors are expecting to see big growth. You’ll need to expand beyond those early adopters to the early majority. And what are THEY shopping for? They want help with a core use case and workflow that is a high value to their business. When you position for these folks, the technology starts to take a backseat. Instead, the goal is to show the customer how you can help them accomplish their core job to be done. “Here’s how we can help you automate filling out security questionnaires.” “Here’s how we can help you generate legal documents automatically.” “Here’s how we can help you research leading up to a negotiation.” The issue for Series A founders is that while their AI agent COULD help with all those workflows… …the marketing budget is still likely too small to fully penetrate each of those different segments. Each of the workflows/JTBD I just mentioned has their own set of competitors, stakeholders, needs, problems, etc. And to be the go-to solution for ANY of them will be extremely challenging… …let alone winning ALL those markets at once with a very small GTM team. And so the difficulty is prioritizing WHICH workflow-based segment to tackle first. What really is the killer use case for your AI Agent product? Vanta started with helping companies get SOC II compliant. Stripe started with handling payments. Notion started with note-taking. Gong started with call recording. What will YOU start with, AI agent company?

In February 2023, this wasn't a popular take. "Agents" didn’t exist as a term outside of research projects. The idea of agentic AI autonomously handling end-to-end enterprise workflows sounded like science fiction. We saw something different and decided to bet on it. Knowledge work is fundamentally composed of decisions, not just tasks. If you could build systems that reason, learn, and act—that make judgment calls, not just follow scripts—you could transform what software was capable of. The early days were humbling. We were building a new category—a horizontal agentic operating system for the enterprise. That meant convincing enterprises to trust AI with critical workflows: support, finance, legal, sales. The skepticism was real. "Can it actually work in production?" The only way to answer: build something that works. Not a demo. A production-grade system with the architectural trust and reliability enterprises demand. What made it possible: the team. I'm often asked what I'm most proud of at Ema. Lead with the problem, not your product ↳ People scroll past features ↳ They stop for pain points 2. Use customer language, not marketing speak ↳ Copy their exact words from sales calls ↳ Drop the jargon completely 4. Show outcomes, not processes ↳ "Close deals 40% faster" beats "streamlined workflow" ↳ Quantify the benefit 5. Keep copy under 100 words ↳ Attention spans are brutal ↳ Every word must earn its place 6. Add one clear CTA ↳ Multiple options kill conversions ↳ Tell them exactly what to do next 7. Target narrow audiences ↳ "Founders" is too broad ↳ "Series A SaaS founders" converts better 8. Front-load your value ↳ Assume nobody reads past line two ↳ Put the win upfront 9. Use social proof strategically ↳ Specific results beat vague testimonials ↳ "Increased MRR by $50K" works 10. Test images vs. text-only posts ↳ Sometimes less design performs better ↳ Let data decide 11. Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: Cloud account managers at AWS, Azure, and Google carry quotas that would make most CEOs sweat - often 💵 $100M+ a year.

Cloud account managers at AWS, Azure, and Google carry quotas that would make most CEOs sweat - often 💵 $100M+ a year. When you ask for their time, you’re competing against hundreds of partners and thousands of accounts. Ask yourself: If you had a $50–200M million quota, how much time would you spend on a pitch with an unclear benefit? That's not cynicism—it's math. And it explains why most first co-sell calls go nowhere. 💎 What actually works: the 5-Minute Intro The formula: Customer outcome → Cloud outcome (specific services) → Your outcome. → What’s your one-liner use case? Skip features and jargon. Focus on customers. “We reduce <metric> by <X%> in <N> weeks for <buyer persona>.” “In <industry/geo>, we delivered <ROI/time-to-value>. Two recent examples: <1> and <2>” → Why should a seller care? Translate how this makes the cloud provider win and tie it to consumption. Does it drive new workloads? New line of business? Expansion of specific services? Helps with their hashtag #AI story? Be explicit about their benefit, not yours. “Workloads run on <Service>. Deal drives < e.g. AWS Bedrock> usage and <> hours.” Emphasize that you transact through the marketplace - tied to their comp, helps customers burn commits, etc. → Who is your sponsor at this customer? Prove you’ve done the work - already have traction in the customer and aren't fishing. → What have you already done? Champion mapped. Security review complete. Buyer path aligned to marketplace workflows. → What’s your ONE ask? Account intel? Customer's spend commitment? Help with POC? Procurement intro? One ask per touch. Many asks = no action 3️⃣ principles that make this stick → About-YOU, not about-me Swap features for customer and cloud benefits. Refine to 2–3 short sentences. → Narrow scope Start with one deal + one vertical + one region + one repeatable use case. Don’t boil the ocean. → Right pitch, right room AEs care about revenue and speed. Engineers care about fit and risk. Procurement cares about time and terms. Keep the outcome constant; adjust the angle. 🛑 What kills your credibility → Generic pitches across clouds Say the wrong service name and you’ve signaled you’re not serious. → Broad account-mapping sessions Unless you’ve already closed together, sellers won’t spend hours theorizing. Show up with a qualified opportunity. → Assuming your sales team “gets it” instinctively Even seasoned enterprise reps need training on hyperscaler economics and marketplace mechanics, if they don’t have previous cloud experience. Most successful alliance leaders personally 🤝 join the first seller calls to frame and de-risk the conversation. Then they hand-hold the first five to ten wins. ֎ CO-SELL Prompt If you want to make this repeatable, I turned it into a Co-Sell Prompt that converts your raw notes into a seller-ready 5-Minute Intro. ⤵️ Comment CO-SELL and I’ll send it to you tomorrow.

Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: Not sure how to leverage AI to actually make money?

Not sure how to leverage AI to actually make money? Here's a 6-part roadmap to $1M with zero employees. (Even getting to your first $1K, $10K, or $50K is worth the effort) Everyone talks about AI changing everything. But nobody shows you the actual playbook or step-by-step process. Here it is: 1. Spot the Pain Find boring, repetitive problems that cost businesses money. → Messy email inboxes → Manual data entry → Repetitive form filling Map the workflow: inputs → steps → outputs 2. Start as a Service Don't automate first. Validate first. Do the work manually as a productized service. Charge from day one. Prove demand before you build automation. 3. Choose Your Business Model • Productized services (fixed scope, fixed price) • Cohorts/courses (time-boxed outcomes) • SaaS/micro-tools (automate one workflow) • Templates/digital assets (recurring passive income) 4. Build Your Marketing Engine LISTEN → Identify pain points CREATE → Build content COMPOUND → Get traffic PERSONALIZE → Tailor experiences 5. Set Up Operations Two rules: → If it repeats: automate it → If it needs judgment: template it Tools: Lindy, Fathom, Cursor, Precision 6. Deploy Your Sales Engine Capture → Qualify → Nurture → Close → Expand Use AI for instant replies and follow-ups. Create decks that show: problem, proof, plan, price. Path to $1M: • $3k/mo × 28 clients = $1M • $1k product × 84/month = $1M • $199 SaaS × 420 subs = $1M Start small. Scale with AI. Full framework 👇 Credit: Chris Donnelly - Step by Step Give him a follow for everything business, LinkedIn growth and AI business growth related topics. The important thing is to keep learning and growing. What's one step you can take today?

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: I now spend more time in Claude Code than Clay.

I now spend more time in Claude Code than Clay. Here are 10 things every GTM operator needs to know to get started: 1. You don’t need to know how to code VS Code + Claude Code extension. Install, sign in, start prompting. If you can use ChatGPT, you can use Claude Code. 2. Always create a CLAUDE.md file first This is your system prompt for the project. Without it, Claude is generic. With it, it becomes an expert in your workflows, tools, and data. 3. Use the WAT Framework Workflows (markdown instructions) → Agent (Claude Code) → Tools (scripts that execute actions). Three folders. Clean and scalable. 4. Plan Mode before you build anything Don’t just prompt and let it rip. Plan Mode makes Claude think harder, ask better questions, and catch gaps you missed. 5. Over-communicate your first prompt Describe the goal AND the features. Claude will ask you questions you hadn’t thought of - that’s the whole point. 6. MCP Servers are your app store Connect to Gmail, Sheets, Slack, your CRM - once. Claude handles all the endpoints and parameters automatically. 7. Skills = reusable knowledge packs Install skill files for design, n8n, data enrichment - whatever you need. Claude loads only what’s relevant per task. 8. Claude fixes its own mistakes Hits an error → investigates → fixes the code → updates the workflow so it never breaks again. Self-healing. 9. Deploy to Modal for ~$0 Push your workflow to the cloud. Pay only when it runs. Schedule triggers or webhook triggers. Runs while you sleep. 10. Run a security review before deploying One prompt: “Check for exposed API keys and vulnerabilities.” 30 seconds. Prevents a nightmare. You don’t need to be a developer to build automations that used to require one. Start with one workflow. Improve it. Deploy it. Then build the next one. P.S: What would you automate first?

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: The entire issue with AI right now is just one thing...

The entire issue with AI right now is just one thing... Trust. It's not cost, or which vendor/provider, or security/compliance issues. For Enterprise users it is literally whether we can trust the output of AI or not. Enterprise use-cases aren't about planning your next vacation to Italy. AI is perfectly good at day-to-day, consumer-grade level answers. But if you ask AI to analyze your product pricing for example, it'll throw more than just a handful of wacky ideas out there. Or if you want ideas on turning a human workflow into an automated one, even if you give it perfect documentation, AI won't spit out exactly what you want. Yeah, sure, AI helps a lot with boiler plate stuff. But half-working software isn't what Enterprise users are going to pay for in the long run. Compare AI to Cloud, for example. What if your VP of CloudOps said, "the product is up & running 80% of the time." Terrible, right? But we're OK with AI doing that. Our bar is so low. If AI spells "strawberry" 🍓 correctly we're clapping as if it solved world hunger. Far too many are caught up in the hype of AI and we're giving the technology a big pass, especially in the Enterprise. And it doesn't matter that AI is non-deterministic. That's a great phrase that unfortunately has come to mean "inaccurate". AI has to do what a business wants/needs, otherwise it has no ROI and is purely a novelty act.

Write a LinkedIn post for devtools about workflow. Angle: Found a project claiming 22x faster than FastAPI.

Found a project claiming 22x faster than FastAPI. The code and benchmarks are fully vibecoded. Every benchmark runs with an in-memory cache that can't be disabled without recompiling. The "NO CACHE" mode still uses the cache. The warmup prewarms the exact IDs the benchmark will hit. ORDER BY random() doesn't help because the cache key ignores it. Full breakdown with links to the code: https://lnkd.in/dtTSrNzt We're in the era of vibecoded projects. Read the source before trusting the numbers. I'm not against LLMs, they're great for research and digging through code. The problem is when people ship vibecoded benchmarks without understanding what the code actually does. UPD: the author blocked me on LinkedIn instead of addressing any of this.

Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: Before you buy another AI tool, map the seven bottlenecks that are actually slowing your marketing down.

Before you buy another AI tool, map the seven bottlenecks that are actually slowing your marketing down. AI is not a magic switch. It is an amplifier. If your workflow is broken, AI just breaks it faster. Whiteboard the entire path from “idea” to “campaign live.” Every handoff, every approval, every tool. Circle the points where work routinely stalls. For each stall point, ask: Is this a data problem? A process problem? Or a people problem? AI is great at pattern-heavy work such as tagging, summarizing, routing and drafting. It is terrible at fixing unclear goals, weak offers or misaligned teams. Do not ask it to do your strategy. Pick one bottleneck. Automate one piece of it. Measure hours saved, error reduction or speed to launch. If it works, document it. Wrap it in a repeatable workflow. Only then should you scale it across campaigns or brands. You do not need more AI. You need cleaner workflows that AI can actually support. Where is the first bottleneck you would map?

Most teams drown in feedback and starve for insight. I’ve felt that pain across CX, SaaS, retail—and especially in gaming, where Discord, reviews, and LiveOps telemetry never sleep. The unlock wasn’t “more data.” It was AI turning feedback → insight → action in hours, not weeks. Here’s what changed for me: Ingest everything, once. Tickets, app reviews, Discord threads, calls, streams—normalized and de-duplicated with PII handled by default. Enrich automatically. LLMs tag topics, intent, and aspect-level sentiment (what players love/hate about this feature in this build). Act where work happens. Copilots draft Jira issues with evidence, propose fixes, and close the loop with customers—human-in-the-loop for quality. Measure what matters. Not just CSAT. In gaming: retention, ARPDAU, event participation. In other industries: conversion, refund rate, cost-to-serve. Gaming example: a balance tweak drops; AI cross-references sentiment from Spanish/Portuguese Discord channels with session logs and flags a difficulty spike for new players on Android. Product gets a one-pager with root cause, repro steps, and a recommended hotfix—before social blows up. That’s the difference between a rocky patch and a win. This isn’t just for studios. Healthcare, fintech, DTC, SaaS—same playbook, different telemetry. I put my approach into a 2025 AI Feedback Playbook: architecture, workflows, guardrails, and a 30/60/90 rollout you can start tomorrow. If you lead Product, CX, Support, or LiveOps, it’s built for you. 👉 I’d love your take—what’s the hardest part of your feedback loop right now? Can Claude Code build a $300K Salesforce project in a single weekend? Spoiler: No. But it did turn me into a 5-person agency. What I did: 1. Task by task, I pushed the code to my org: custom objects, fields, flows, Apex, data migration, even a crawler to load web pages into Data Cloud RAG. Normally, this takes a Business Analyst, a Tech Lead, two Devs, and a QA. Each doing his part. I did it solo. Erik Brynjolfsson is right on: "What's changing isn't just the tools, it's the scale at which a single individual can operate." And I still have a team. We looked at ~30 startups with breakout revenue or traffic growth, and the data points to four themes where customer demand is exploding right now: Agentic Workflows are replacing SaaS Startups are building AI "teammates" and agents that own entire workflows, from SEO to recruiting. The ROI is immediate and the market is rewarding it with real revenue. Generative Tools becoming Pro-Creative Engines GenAI is moving from a fun toy to a serious creative and marketing platform. Startups are seeing big user growth by empowering both pros and a massive long-tail of creators to build high-quality content and businesses. most people run 1 claude code session at a time. with this setup you can run unlimited instances from one place. and they never ask for permission. here's how to 10x your claude code setup in 4 steps: ◉ step 1: install cmux go to cmux.dev and download it. cmux is a terminal built for running multiple ai agents side by side. named workspaces. color-coded. you can send tasks to any of them from anywhere. think of it as mission control for claude code. ◉ step 2: ask claude code what your categories should be don't copy my setup. yours should be different. open claude code and paste this: "look at my conversation history, my project files, and my .claude/ folder. based on what i actually work on, suggest 6 workspace categories for a daily command center. for each one give me: a name, a color, an icon, what context it should pre-load, and what initial prompt it should receive when it launches. format it as a numbered list." claude will analyze your actual workflow and give you personalized categories. maybe yours is: code review, docs, testing, devops, research, slack. maybe it's: sales, hiring, product, marketing, support, analytics. it depends on what you actually do every day. ◉ step 3: build it once you have your 6 categories, paste this: "i want you to create a command center using cmux. run cmux --help to learn every command available. create a file at .claude/scripts/command-center.sh that launches these 6 workspaces: [paste your 6 categories here] each workspace should: - run claude --dangerously-skip-permissions - get a colored status pill in the sidebar - get pinned so it stays visible - get reordered to the top of the sidebar - receive an initial prompt that loads the right context for its role also create a dispatch.sh so i can send a task to any workspace by name. also create a command-center-close.sh that only closes these 6 workspaces and leaves everything else untouched." that's it. claude reads cmux --help, figures out every command, and builds the entire system for you. you don't write a single line of bash. ◉ step 4: launch and dispatch you can ask claude code to do that or you can do it yourself: bash .claude/scripts/command-center.sh 30 seconds later you have 6 agents in your sidebar. all running. all skip permissions. all pre-loaded with context. then you just dispatch: bash .claude/scripts/dispatch.sh Content "write a post about cmux" bash .claude/scripts/dispatch.sh Outreach "research this prospect" bash .claude/scripts/dispatch.sh Clients "status update on all active clients" what changes after this? you stop: - context-switching between tasks. - re-explaining who you are every session. - waiting for one thing to finish before starting the next. so what ? each agent has its role. each agent has its context. each agent runs without interrupting you. the setup takes 5 minutes. the productivity gain is permanent. comment "cmux" for full setup. repost if you think it might help someone.

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: An AI agent constructed a knowledge graph of my AI strategy frameworks from my Substack archives in 5 hours with no API costs, frontier model, or cloud.

An AI agent constructed a knowledge graph of my AI strategy frameworks from my Substack archives in 5 hours with no API costs, frontier model, or cloud. Just a Dell Pro Precision with an RTX 6000 and 128 GB of RAM heating my office. The hardware and open-source LLMs aren't the story. Most agentic AI projects fail for the same reason most automation projects failed. No one defines the work that the technology is supposed to do. Teams hand a capable machine an undefined job and are surprised when the result is undefined. What did we expect? Here's what I learned after I started building agents locally: The workflow design or reorchestration comes first. The agent comes second. Information scientists have known this since the 1970s. They start by designing the schema (entity types, relationships, constraints, and validation rules). Then they define the vocabulary of the output before they have even touched the inputs. I did the same thing. 13 entity types, 16 relationship types, and a multi-pass extraction pipeline encoded as a LangGraph state machine. The agent never makes a design decision. Those are made by experts during workflow specification. The result is a reusable framework that works for knowledge graphs, contract review, competitive intelligence, research synthesis, or any domain where the work can be decomposed into defined passes with structured inputs and outputs. The uncomfortable implication is that the bottleneck in agentic AI isn't model capability. It's whether your organization can define its own work or customer workflows precisely enough for an agent to execute them. Full breakdown in the article; the second in a series. https://lnkd.in/g7ajFHdc #DellProPrecision

After 22 years in cybersecurity, I've realized most security tools are designed to do one thing: find problems. Today, we're launching a platform built to actually solve them. I'm thrilled to announce that I've started a new position as Co-Founder & CEO at SpartanX.ai, which officially comes out of stealth today! This isn't just another venture for me; it's the culmination of everything I've learned about the deep, systemic friction between security and development. For decades, we've been caught in a cycle: we buy tools that find thousands of vulnerabilities, creating a mountain of noisy alerts and manual work. The result is burnout for our teams and critical breaches for our businesses. Why now? The rise of Agentic AI allows us to move beyond this broken model. At SpartanX, we've built an AI-powered command center that uses autonomous agents to automate the entire security workflow. This is a force multiplier for your teams, handling everything from the complex to the mundane. Our agents: Automate the full vulnerability lifecycle: They investigate, validate, eliminate false positives, and generate the code fix. Automate security operations: They execute thousands of day-to-day tasks through a pre-built library, from generating compliance reports to running complex threat analyses. This is a game-changer for: - Security Teams: Who can automate their operational playbook and finally focus on strategic defense, not just triage. - Development Teams: Who get AI-generated patches and automated pull requests, turning hours of patching into a simple code review. If I'm going to tell customers that AI will transform how their teams work, I should be able to show them what that actually looks like inside a company running the experiment. So here's ours — unvarnished, with real numbers, including the places where the results surprised us. Freshworks builds software for customer support, IT, HR, and sales teams. We also have those teams — and we've been systematically deploying AI across all of them. That makes us something most commentators in this debate aren't: an actual data source. Here's what we're seeing. AI is a multiplier. It's not a replacement. In engineering, over the last two months alone, our team has generated 13 million lines of code and handled 26 million AI queries internally. Engineers still own every architectural decision and everything that ships. What AI changed is the pace of the work nobody loves — boilerplate, test generation, navigating codebases no single person fully holds in their head. The result isn't fewer engineers. It's engineers doing more of what actually requires an engineer. In HR, AI handles onboarding, offboarding, documentation, and performance management workflows. HR Ops inquiries dropped ~33%. Our hiring team runs an AI sourcing agent that evaluates 14,000 applicants every week in real time — and hiring cycles are 30% faster. The team isn't smaller. In marketing, the full campaign cycle moves faster and the team uses AI for everything from product demo voiceovers to accelerating campaign development; from idea to landing page copy and development. What 35 case studies taught us: Our engineering team documented 35 internal case studies this quarter, places where AI measurably improved productivity, code quality, or delivery speed. Nobody mandated that documentation. Teams did it because the results were worth capturing. The pattern is consistent: AI works where the workflow is clear, the output is measurable, and a human remains accountable for the outcome. It doesn't work as a shortcut around judgment. Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: Enterprises need to stop calling these things “autonomous AI agents.” Most of what I’m seeing in the market is agent theater: a thin orchestration script wrapped around a remote LLM (ChatGPT/Gemini/Grok/etc.), plus a few tools, plus a dashboard.

Enterprises need to stop calling these things “autonomous AI agents.” Most of what I’m seeing in the market is agent theater: a thin orchestration script wrapped around a remote LLM (ChatGPT/Gemini/Grok/etc.), plus a few tools, plus a dashboard. It looks impressive in a demo. It falls apart the moment you treat it like real infrastructure. Let’s be blunt: if the “agent” can’t function when the network degrades, the provider rate-limits you, the model behavior changes, or an API policy blocks a step, then it’s not autonomous. It’s a puppet. The cloud LLM is the hand, and your “agent” is just the strings. Why are enterprises building them this way anyway? Because it’s the fastest way to ship something that sounds strategic: “Look, we deployed agents.” “Look, it’s agentic.” “Look, it’s autonomous.” But underneath the marketing gloss is a fragile architecture with all the classic enterprise failure modes: latency spikes, outages, unpredictable behavior drift, data residency headaches, compliance exposure, runaway costs, and a single external dependency that quietly becomes your control plane for decision-making. If you actually want value from agentic systems, build real agents: systems with local execution, durable memory, owned state, verifiable actions, and graceful degradation. Use remote models as an augmentation layer, not as the only brain the system has. Put deterministic guardrails in front of actions. Treat “reasoning” like a component you engineer, test, observe, and control—not something you rent and pray over. Stop deploying “agents” that are one provider outage away from becoming inert workflow scripts. Call it what it is: automation with outsourced cognition. If your agent’s IQ lives on somebody else’s endpoint, you didn’t build autonomy—you built dependency. What are you seeing in your org: real agent engineering… or agent theater? #AI #EnterpriseArchitecture #AgenticAI #CloudComputing

Write a LinkedIn post for cybersecurity about workflow. Angle: When High-Tech Security Meets High-Touch Therapy My wife and I work in vastly different professions, and our daily grind usually has very little in common.

When High-Tech Security Meets High-Touch Therapy My wife and I work in vastly different professions, and our daily grind usually has very little in common. In my job as a CISO, my world is purely digital. I spend my time dealing with hackers, protecting data, and using AI to stay ahead of cyber threats. It’s a fast-paced environment where everything happens behind a screen. Beyond just keeping the "bad guys" out, my main goal is to ensure the business stays up and running and that our services are always available when needed. My wife’s world as a Pediatric OT (Occupational Therapist) practice owner is the opposite. Occupational Therapy is traditionally Low-Tech. It is a "high-touch" field built on physical interaction, sensory bins, and manual progress tracking. It’s human-centric in its purest, most analog form. One of the biggest hurdles she faces is continuity of care. When a therapy session is over, and a home program is prescribed, progress often stalls. Family follow-through is a major hindrance; without the right tools at home, even the best treatment plan can lose its effectiveness. We recently started using Korro.ai (an AI-driven pediatric therapy platform) in her practice, and it was a rare moment where our professional worlds aligned. It didn't necessarily "shift" my thinking, but it solidified my belief that AI can be so much more than just automation and agentic workflows. This experience brought the real potential of AI into focus for me: Beyond the "Shield and Sword": In cybersecurity, we talk about AI as a weapon or a shield. Here, I saw it used as a bridge. It leverages computer vision to turn rigorous therapy into an immersive "game" for kids, helping close the engagement gap at home. Precision meets Play: It brings the clinical precision of my world (data tracking and micro-analysis) into her world of swings and balance beams. It doesn't replace the therapist; it empowers them with data that the human eye simply can’t capture in real-time. The Real Worth of AI: Seeing AI applied to a traditionally non-tech field to help a child reach a developmental milestone, that is the true value of this technology. As a CISO, I’m used to AI that protects systems. As a partner to an OT, I’m now seeing AI that has the potential to transform lives. We talk a lot about AI for ROI and automation, but real progress happens when we use that precision to bridge the gap between a plan and a person actually reaching their potential. Curious to know, what’s one example you’ve come across where AI helped a person truly reach theirs? Shefali Desai, MA OTR/L #AI #HealthTech #CyberSecurity #OccupationalTherapy #Innovation2026

The question security leaders are increasingly being asked is no longer: “Are we secure?” It’s this: “How do we know what’s real, who (or what) is acting, and what they’re allowed to do — at machine speed?” 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗲𝘅𝗶𝘀𝘁𝘀 Three shifts are converging at once: ◉ 𝗧𝗿𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗮𝗽𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 Synthetic content can now trigger fraud, confusion, and bad decisions at scale. ◉ 𝗜𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗶𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗿𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗽𝗮𝘁𝗵 Tokens, sessions, OAuth grants, and non-human identities are becoming the real attack surface. ◉ 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝘆 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝗿𝗮𝗱𝗶𝘂𝘀 Agents don’t just expose data, they can take actions (and cause “authorized harm”). 𝗖𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗧𝗼𝗽 𝗧𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗱𝘀 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟲–𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟴: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗡𝗲𝘄 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗹 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗲𝘀 — 𝗧𝗿𝘂𝘀𝘁, 𝗜𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘆, 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝘆, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗜𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗣𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗿 This is not a “tools list.” It’s a 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹 𝗹𝗲𝗻𝘀 for leaders deciding what must be engineered into architecture + operating model (not bolted on later). 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗶𝗲𝗰𝗲 (𝟯𝟬 𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗱𝘀) ✅ Digital trust, fraud, and provenance ✅ LLM / GenAI app security (prompt injection, insecure output handling, prompt leakage) ✅ AI infrastructure security (context supply chain, retrieval authorization, ingestion poisoning, embeddings, orchestration integrity) ✅ Agent autonomy and workflow integrity (agent sprawl, excessive agency, memory/state compromise, agent-to-agent lateral movement) ✅ Shadow AI, malware delivery through agents, software supply chain shifts, AI compute monetization attacks ✅ Identity-first intrusions, OAuth/integration abuse, NHI explosion, machine-speed cyber ops, and PQC/confidential compute/NTN deadlines 𝗠𝘆 𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗶𝘀 We need to stop optimizing for the 𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗮𝗱𝗲’𝘀 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗿 and start engineering the 𝗻𝗲𝘄 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗹 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗲𝘀: ◉ 𝗧𝗿𝘂𝘀𝘁 → provenance, signed approvals, tamper-evident evidence ◉ 𝗜𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘆 → session lifecycle, OAuth governance, NHI discipline ◉ 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝘆 → permissioned context, runtime policy gates, reversible actions If you’re a CISO / CIO / CTO / Risk leader / AI platform leader, this is the design conversation that matters now. Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: A real-life, end-to-end use case of MCPs in a GTM workflow: Combine multiple MCPs to research + write a personalized outreach message In this workflow, I use 3 MCPs: - Firecrawl for crawling website content - Tavily for searching the web - A custom MCP developed by The Workflow Company for fetching profile and posts data In addition to these MCPs, I use Claude as the LLM to research a target lead's LinkedIn activity and write a personalized and relevant outreach DM.

A real-life, end-to-end use case of MCPs in a GTM workflow: Combine multiple MCPs to research + write a personalized outreach message In this workflow, I use 3 MCPs: - Firecrawl for crawling website content - Tavily for searching the web - A custom MCP developed by The Workflow Company for fetching profile and posts data In addition to these MCPs, I use Claude as the LLM to research a target lead's LinkedIn activity and write a personalized and relevant outreach DM. The series of steps taken are: 1. Crawl the content on our website (Firecrawl MCP) 2. Extract previous LinkedIn posts written by myself (WFCO MCP) 3. Combine 1 and 2 to create a detailed overview of our business model (Claude) 4. Find a potential target lead (Tavily MCP) 5. Extract the target's LinkedIn posts (WFCO MCP) 6. Write a personalized and relevant outreach message (Claude) 7. Convert the the conversation I just had with Claude into a prompt so I can repeat and scale the message generation process There's a lot more that would go into a production-ready workflow—like including a decision engine to determine whether a lead is a good fit—but I wanted to share one small use case of leveraging MCPs for a GTM workflow today.

Write a LinkedIn post for devtools about workflow. Angle: Debugging performance issues doesn’t have to feel like guesswork.

Debugging performance issues doesn’t have to feel like guesswork. Here’s a simple workflow I found powerful: 1. Open Chrome DevTools 2. Go to the Memory tab 3. Take a heap snapshot and download it 4. Drop it into Cursor Cursor can generate Python scripts to analyze the snapshot and highlight what’s actually slowing your site down. Instead of manually digging through thousands of objects, you let AI: • Identify memory-heavy constructors • Spot unusual object growth • Surface potential leaks • Explain what’s happening in plain English This is a great example of using AI as a diagnostic tool, not just a code generator. We’re moving from: “Why is my site slow?” to “Here’s the exact memory structure causing the issue.” AI-assisted debugging is going to become standard workflow for frontend and full-stack engineers. If you want more practical AI workflows like this (real use cases, not hype): 👉 https://lnkd.in/dybiSH2g ♻️ Share with someone fighting performance issues #WebPerformance #Frontend #Cursor #AIForDevelopers #SoftwareEngineering

The conventional take is that startups are where you lose your job in a down market (e.g. runway dries up, pivots happen, the big guys win). I get it. But I *think* that calculus has flipped. Large orgs cutting headcount are betting AI closes the gap. The logic tracks, but executing that inside a 5,000-person company is slow, political, and messy. At best, you get addition by subtraction. Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: This is not a flashy B2C product about an AI agent automating your sales workflow of your startup.

This is not a flashy B2C product about an AI agent automating your sales workflow of your startup. This is the deep infrastructure needed to safely deploy autonomous AI in the enterprise. I’ve just recorded a Loom video breaking down exactly how NeriLabs solves one of the biggest bottlenecks for deploying AI agents in production: the liability of hallucinations and rogue tool-calls. In the video, I use these Figma mockups to illustrate two critical infrastructure layers we've built: V1: The Hard-Block Action Firewall Think of this as a security perimeter sitting natively inside your VPC. When an AI agent attempts to execute a destructive or non-compliant command, our proxy physically intercepts and severs the connection before it can execute. Because it runs locally, your data never leaves your secure environment. V2: The Self-Healing Proxy with Context Feeding While V1 provides security, V2 guarantees uptime. When the firewall blocks an action, the proxy pings your local context vault (ChromaDB) to retrieve the relevant internal SOPs or API docs. It silently feeds this exact missing context back into the LLM, forcing the agent to correct its own mistake in milliseconds without the end-user ever seeing a glitch. Here is what this architecture actually looks like in production: Healthcare & HIPAA Compliance: An agent attempts to send an outbound API payload containing raw patient diagnosis data. NeriLabs intercepts the PHI leak, blocks the egress, and forces the LLM to redact the payload. • The ROI: Bypass 4–6 months of external Infosec audits and save an estimated $80,000+ in third-party compliance reviews. SaaS Operations & Billing: An agent hallucinates and attempts to issue an unrecoverable $15,000 refund through Stripe. NeriLabs intercepts the API call, blocks the transaction, and forces the agent to create a manual review ticket instead. • The ROI: Eliminates instant 5-figure financial liabilities and saves 40+ engineering hours per deployment by replacing custom sandbox logic. Cloud IT & DevOps: An internal agent gets confused and attempts to execute a destructive DROP command on a production database instead of the test environment. NeriLabs blocks the production command, injects the missing environment context, and redirects the action to the safe test environment. • The ROI: Averages $300,000 saved in prevented downtime and averts 12+ hours of emergency rollback engineering. Our core thesis is that while RAG solves the data context problem, enterprise AI is bottlenecked by the execution layer. You need deterministic, network level guardrails to safely translate probabilistic LLM outputs into automated infrastructure commands. Curious about how an Action Firewall can secure your agent execution? Comment 'DEMO' or send me a DM, and I'd love to show you a live product demo of the architecture.

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: Your COO is drowning in tech decisions they shouldn't be making.

Your COO is drowning in tech decisions they shouldn't be making. And it's killing execution. Here's the pattern I keep seeing in 10-100 person companies: No dedicated CTO or CIO. The COO becomes the default tech overseer because they "get" the business pain points better than anyone. They know the plant floor needs to ditch paper. They see the workflow bottlenecks. They understand what's actually broken. But tech isn't their core expertise. 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗛𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘀: The COO is already juggling operations, supply chain, people management, budgets, and strategy execution. Now add: tech architecture, cybersecurity, cloud decisions, AI implementation. → Divided attention means shallow dives and band-aid fixes → Lack of tech fluency leads to wrong tool choices → Can't effectively hold technical teams accountable → Projects stall when the COO gets pulled into the next crisis Teams sense the lack of oversight. They deprioritize. Issues get hidden. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗘𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘀: This works okay under 15 people. But as you scale: → Execution slows → Costs creep up → Innovation lags → The COO burns out → Tech becomes reactive instead of strategic 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀: Separate the roles. Bring in a CTO (or fractional CTO) for tech ownership. COO collaborates as the "how do we operate it?" partner. CTO handles "how do we build it?" Clear swim lanes. Regular alignment meetings. Use consultants or advisors so the COO doesn't micromanage technical decisions. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗼𝘁𝘁𝗼𝗺 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗲: Your COO is great at operations. That's what you hired them for. Making them own tech strategy on top of everything else doesn't save money, it costs you in failed projects, slow execution, and burnout. Full breakdown in this week's newsletter. Link in comments 👇 Is your COO stuck owning tech decisions they shouldn't be making?

Write a LinkedIn post for recruiting about workflow. Angle: AI belongs in recruiting.

AI belongs in recruiting. But not everywhere. Definitely use it for: 1. Sourcing. Finding candidates who match a profile across millions of data points. AI is better at this than humans. Faster, more thorough, no fatigue. 2. Outreach. Relevant messages at scale. Following up. Scheduling. All the coordination work that eats up a recruiter's day. 3. Pipeline management. Tracking candidates, updating statuses, surfacing who needs attention. Workflow automation, basically. Definitely DO NOT use it for: Screening calls with high-caliber, passive candidates. If you're reaching out to someone who's in the top 1% of what they do, they don't want to talk to an AI. They want to talk to another human who's in the top 1% at what they do (at least in 2026). An AI video screen for outbound recruiting is insulting. It signals that you don't value their time enough to put a real person in front of them. Inbound applications? Sure, AI screening has a place. You're dealing with volume. But outbound to elite talent? That's human-to-human. It has to be. We’re going to see more and more recruiters automate everything and wonder why their candidate experience sucks and their close rates tank.

I've been hearing these same 3 K12 GTM and prospecting mistakes a lot in January. And it's not just new founders; they've come up in convos from pre-seed to established growth-stage orgs. In no particular order: 1. Using Apollo for email warmup (or anything K-12 related) If you're warming up domains, switch to Warmy.io - Email channel. Reliable., or use Instantly.ai, lemlist, or EmailBison native warmup pool. We prefer Bison bc it's private, but it's geared more towards agency-style businesses like us so might not be a good fit. And for education contact data specifically, talk to Chris at Getedulead. We leverage EduLead's data and then verify/custom scrape using Clay workflows. This helps us fill in gaps and find hard to find contacts. LMK if you need help with this (TAM to Target is the only K12/HigherEd-focused Clay Solution Partner out there). 2. Sending cold emails from HubSpot First off it's a terms violation. So you might end up in HubSpot jail. Use HubSpot for marketing emails and newsletters bc that's what it's built for. For cold infrastructure, connect with Anthony Baltodano at Mission Inbox, then run sequences through Instantly (email only) or Lemlist (phone, LI, email). 3. Scaling outbound before your inbound funnel is ready Here's what people miss: outbound generates inbound. Prospects get your email, look you up, hit your website. It's a buyer-led journey out there! If that site isn't ready to convert, you're wasting those leads. We see this constantly. Like literally every week. Clients get an inbound spike and think it's organic, but it's actually attribution from outbound campaigns. (Side note - that's why syncing up all your email campaigns to Hubspot is essential - it's why we partner with OutboundSync. So basically you need to fix your landing pages and conversion paths first before you go outbound. Or at minimum, build them in parallel. I think these are pretty quick wins. Maybe the landing pages will take some marketing effort, but the rest is just execution shift. I go into a bit more detail below 👇 Let me know if you're working through any of this or need help!

Just published our new preprint on TechRxiv and ResearchGate: CVE Semantic: Learning Logic Vulnerability Patterns through Semantic Analysis of CVE Fix Differentials by Me, Vansh Thakkar, Dev Desai and Harsh Mahesh Tikone DOI: 10.36227/techrxiv.176801002.20103410/v1 A lot of vulnerability detection still relies on syntactic patterns, which often miss the real issue: the logic change between vulnerable and fixed code. Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: DEVOPS, DEVOPS, DEVOPS Devops is the most important function in the age of AI.

DEVOPS, DEVOPS, DEVOPS Devops is the most important function in the age of AI. You don't need 10x engineers. You need 10x devops engineers. If you're a founder, focus on hiring the best devops folks you can. To me this feels obvious, but I don't hear founders talking about it enough. I hear all the time from founders "I need to hire more AI native engineers". I rarely hear "I need to hire one 10x devops engineer". Maybe that's a subtle distinction, but it's important. Why? Agents are doing more coding than ever. Getting agents to write code is trivial. The hard part is setting up the right environment and guardrails to make sure they write the correct code and accidently wreak havoc. The hard part is to make sure a single engineer can spin up 10x+ agents in a few minutes to run in parallel. For that, you need devops. Stripe wrote a post about how: "1,300 Stripe pull requests merged each week are completely minion produced, human-reviewed, but containing no human-written code." The post is entirely about the devops infrastructure that makes this possible using: - devboxes (cloud based dev environments) - blueprints (LLM orchestration and workflows) - MCP servers and more The post is not about how "we hired 10x AI native engineers". Tesla's product isn't cars, it's the automated factory to build the cars (now robots?). If you're building software in the age of AI, you need a software factory and for that you need devops.

AI Engineering career paths. Most people don't know which one to pick. Firstly, you start by learning AI engineering in general, then once you are done learning it you can pick a niche. Here's a breakdown so you can choose yours: Direction 1: AI Product Engineer Best if you want startup jobs fast You build AI-powered products that real users interact with. Focus on: LLM apps, RAG, agents, deployment, product UX This is the most common path. If you already have software engineering skills, you're closer than you think. Direction 2: Applied ML / LLM Engineer Best if you want deeper technical roles You go beyond API calls and understand what's happening under the hood. Focus on: fine-tuning, when to fine-tune vs prompt, evaluation, inference optimization, open-source models, training pipelines The most important decision in this path, do you need to change the model, or just change how you talk to it? Direction 3: AI Automation Engineer Best if you want to build for businesses immediately Less about building products, more about solving operational problems with AI. Focus on: workflow orchestration, business process automation, multi-tool systems, CRM, docs, email, support, and ops use cases Real business automation is almost never one LLM call. It's chains of actions across multiple systems. Pick one. Go deep. Practice. Everything in AI engineering is best learned by building, not just reading. Which direction are you taking? Write a LinkedIn post for healthcare about workflow. Angle: I walked one of the biggest health tech show floors in the country.

I walked one of the biggest health tech show floors in the country. ViVE 2026 in LA. Over 7,000 executives. AI everywhere. Every booth. Every panel. Every hallway conversation. And the question I can't stop thinking about: How many of these companies will be here in 2029? I'm not being cynical. I'm being realistic. Here's the tension nobody wants to say out loud: The AI space in the exhibit hall was massive. Point solutions are everywhere — prior auth automation, clinical decision support, patient engagement, revenue cycle, you name it. But adoption inside health systems is still slow. Budgets are tight. Someone pulled me aside and said: "Drew — we built a tool just like Company X's product in two days." Two days. So here's the question every startup at ViVE should be asking themselves: If an entrenched EHR vendor or a well-resourced health system can replicate your point solution in a sprint, what's your actual moat? And here's the question the entrenched players should be asking: If you can build it in two days, why haven't you shipped it in two years? Being nimble isn't just about speed to build. It's about speed to implement, speed to iterate, and speed to earn clinician trust. Some of the companies I saw this week will define the next era of healthcare. And some of them are already dead — they just don't know it yet. The ones who survive won't be the ones with the best demo. They'll be the ones who understood that healthcare doesn't buy technology. Healthcare buys trust. Workflow integration. Clinical validation. And patience. Interesting times.

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: The 5 Hard Problems Engineering and Manufacturing Teams Still Face in 2026 One of my readers recently commented: “AI is not a strategy.

The 5 Hard Problems Engineering and Manufacturing Teams Still Face in 2026 One of my readers recently commented: “AI is not a strategy. It is an instrument.” I liked that observation. Right now the number of “AI for X” solutions is exploding. Every week there is a new tool promising to transform engineering, design, or manufacturing. But instead of starting with tools, I decided to start the week with something more fundamental: the problems engineering teams still face every day. Over the past two decades, CAD and PLM vendors have significantly improved their products. We went through the cloud transformation, followed by the mobile wave, and today engineering teams have more powerful tools than ever before. And yet many operational problems stubbornly remain. Here are five that show up again and again: 1️⃣ Design data still lives in files that are hard to manage and access. 2️⃣ Product information is fragmented across mechanical, electronics, and software systems. 3️⃣ Supply chains are now part of engineering decisions. 4️⃣ ERP integrations remain painful. 5️⃣ Collaboration with contract manufacturers is often slow and manual. The pattern is surprisingly consistent: product complexity is growing faster than the systems used to manage it. In my new article, I explore why these problems still exist in 2026 despite decades of investment in PLM, ERP, and cloud software. While everyone is talking about AI, the next transition is already becoming visible — from cloud systems toward AI-driven engineering workflows. But if we don’t understand the underlying problems first, we risk repeating the same mistakes we made during the cloud and mobile transitions. Full article: https://lnkd.in/erwqXHeU Curious to hear from others — what problems are engineering teams in your organization struggling with the most today? Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: Tried something different this weekend: building almost entirely from mobile 📱 I stayed off my laptop as much as possible and worked through cloud coding agents, and GitHub with actions.

Tried something different this weekend: building almost entirely from mobile 📱 I stayed off my laptop as much as possible and worked through cloud coding agents, and GitHub with actions. I focused more on defining objectives and reviewing outcomes than on directly tuning code. I did not start with a concrete app idea. I started with a raw data source I wanted to explore, thinking a visual experience might help surface what was actually interesting inside it. That meant I jumped into the problem space quickly, defined the agent instructions early, and worked with very little upfront context. That created a very different loop: define the next objective, let an agent build, review from mobile, refine, repeat. A few things became clear quickly. Tool differences matter more than I expected when you are mobile-only. PR creation and update behavior can pull you back to a laptop faster than expected when they do not work smoothly. Most of all, merge conflicts become a much bigger problem when you have multiple agents moving in parallel. The biggest lesson, though, was process-related. I should have started with deployment to a preview or ephemeral environment from the very beginning. When I was working from my phone, the goal was not deep code inspection first. The goal was fast visibility into the outcome so I could guide the next iteration with confidence. Other lessons: - Define the agent instructions early, obvious perhaps, but what you include at the start matters for mobile (e.g. including deterministic tests) - If the build is visual, require screenshots for every page during each iteration, ideally generated from the end-to-end test suite - Treat PR workflow reliability as part of the tool choice, because merge conflict cleanup is not trivial on mobile Curious how others are handling this. If you are using cloud agents away from your desk, what guidance or workflow has made the biggest difference?

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: Two weeks ago I shared what AIP-99 brings to Apache Airflow 3: @task.agent, @task.llm, toolsets, the full decorator suite.

Two weeks ago I shared what AIP-99 brings to Apache Airflow 3: @task.agent, @task.llm, toolsets, the full decorator suite. One thing I didn't call out is: you are not locked into those decorators or Operators. You can use PydanticAI directly like the image in the post shows. Create an agent, pass in Airflow toolsets, call run_sync(). Works from a plain @task, PythonOperator, or any custom operator. The toolsets (SQLToolset, HookToolset, MCPToolset) resolve Airflow connections lazily through BaseHook.get_connection(). Credentials come from Airflow's secret backend. No hardcoded keys. This matters when you want full control over the agent lifecycle: - Run multiple agent calls in one task - Swap models at runtime - Combine outputs from different agents before returning If you want the guardrails (durable execution with step-level caching, HITL review, automatic tool logging), use @task.agent. If you want raw control, just use Pydantic AI directly. Both paths use the same toolsets. Docs PR is up. Link in the comments. #ApacheAirflow #PydanticAI #DataEngineering

Write a LinkedIn post for data about workflow. Angle: Most R&D teams are bolting AI onto processes that were already broken.

Most R&D teams are bolting AI onto processes that were already broken. New discovery agent here. AI code assistant there. Another dashboard nobody checks. Then make sure you update Productboard/JIRA/this deck/dash/doc/stakeholder 🫠 [insert meme of Ben Affleck smoking] Same fragmented workflow underneath. I keep coming back to a simple frame for what AI-native R&D actually looks like: Know: Are you solving the right problem? Not the loudest customer. Not a pet feature. The right problem, backed by real signal from real conversations, support tickets, and usage data. Flow: Can you build and ship fast? Not just write code fast. The whole loop, without the handoff tax, the context-switching, the “waiting on alignment” stalls. And do it with top tier taste and craft. Go: Does your launch actually land? The best product work dies in a mediocre GTM. Positioning, enablement, support docs - that’s not someone else’s job, it's part of the product. It's my patented Know, Flow, Go system for AI-native R&D and can be all yours if you like, comment "KFG" and follow me then send me $50,000. (jk) Here’s the pattern I keep seeing: Teams try to upgrade all three at once. They bolt on AI across every layer and call it transformation. The teams making real progress pick ONE. They rethink that layer from scratch with AI as the foundation, not the feature. Then it pulls the others forward. Better Know means you’re not wasting Flow on the wrong thing. Faster Flow means Go has something real to work with. Stronger Go feeds signal back into Know. You don’t need ten new tools bolted onto your old process. You need one honest rethink of where you’re actually broken. Start there. Go deep. The rest follows. I'm officially joining the LinkedIn thought leadership illuminati with this post. An AI infra startup was about to kill outbound. 21 days later, one AI lead magnet added $780K in pipeline. Founder hated cold calls, cold email, generic steps. Team pushed for inbound, brand, content. Revenue team needed pipeline fast. We offered a test. No big retainer. No long deal. One AI lead magnet plugged into outbound. We mapped: → Stack they use today → Problems they feel now → Recent hiring and funding triggers → Tools already inside the team Then we pulled data with Clay. We joined signals from: → Similarweb → Job boards → Tech trackers → Social data → Hidden “dark” data on the web Next step: segment, not blast. We broke the market into four clean groups. Each group got: → Different lead magnet angle → New subject line → Own follow up flow → Own handoff path to sales We used AI, but we did not trust it alone. AI wrote first drafts. We fixed story, tone, and proof. AI scored replies too. Sales team saw only: → High intent → Clear pain → Right seniority No more “maybe” replies in the main inbox. Result after 21 days: → 1 AI lead magnet → 4 sharp segments → $780K in pipeline → Founder now wants to scale outbound, not kill it AI did not replace outbound. We used AI to make each touch sharp, fast, and clear. If your B2B team wants to test one AI lead magnet with outbound, we can share the full workflow step by step. Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: Everyone is obsessed with optimization.

Everyone is obsessed with optimization. This hack for a 10% lift. This tweak to conversion. This AI workflow to improve efficiency. And yes - optimization matters. But true growth won’t come from just a collection of small improvements stacked together. I believe there are three stages of marketing maturity: 1. Activity 2. Optimization 3. Narrative Stage 1 is activity. Do more. Launch more. Produce more. Teams look busy, but not necessarily strategic. A lot of startups live here longer than they should. Stage 2 is optimization - this is where most teams stall. They get better at targeting, segmentation, reporting. They improve performance. But they never decide what they want to be known for. And without that clarity? All the hacks eventually lose impact. The real leap is Stage 3: building a narrative. Owning the story. Defining what you stand for. Choosing what you’re willing to ignore. That’s when marketing shifts from execution to influence. Without narrative, optimization is just motion. With narrative, it compounds.

Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: AI Founders: If you have room for one more New Year’s resolution, add Strategic Framing.

AI Founders: If you have room for one more New Year’s resolution, add Strategic Framing. Seeing too many AI companies with better tech and stronger execution lose momentum because the founder can’t clearly explain what the company stands for, where it’s going, or why it should exist. This isn’t a marketing problem. It’s a leadership one. The CEO is the primary narrator. If you can’t articulate a clear founder narrative - how you think, why the world is changing, and why your company matters - no deck, consultant, or rebrand will fix it. For customers, you’re asking them to abandon incumbents and adopt new workflows and business models. Clear framing makes change feel inevitable instead of risky. For investors, especially at seed and Series A, most pitches sound the same as trends rotate. What cuts through is a sharp point of view and a credible long-term vision. For hiring, the best AI engineers and operators don’t just join for total comp or valuations. They want to join category defining companies. Your story is the strategy. Seeing founders wanting to outsource this - that's a mistake. Make this a priority — it takes time, especially if it doesn’t come naturally to you. Avoid generic startup marketing help, and instead seek out experts, founders, investors, and operators who have built category-defining companies. Start writing 1–2 page internal memos and pressure-test your framing with a wider audience, say a smart non-expert, a great engineer outside your domain, or an operator. If they can’t repeat it back clearly, you don’t have it yet. Storytelling won’t save a bad product. But for strong teams, it’s a force multiplier.

The most significant shift in enterprise AI strategy I’ve seen yet just showed up in our newest CIO data. In my latest Futurum 1H Global Decision Maker Survey, the traditional AI narrative has clearly broken. CIO priorities are pivoting from productivity → innovation. The early “smart chatbot” era of enterprise AI is already being surpassed as organizations begin focusing on what AI enables them to strategically create, not just what it can accelerate. Another massive signal in the data: The pilot era is ending. Pilot-stage AI adoption has dropped to just 31%, while 74% of CIOs now report having well-formed AI implementation plans. At the same time, AI has more than doubled as a top IT spending category in just one year. Enterprise AI is no longer primarily an experiment—it has moved decisively into execution. Where AI is being applied inside organizations is shifting as well. AI adoption in R&D nearly tripled, rising from 9.9% to 27.9%, while sales-focused AI declined by 18 points. CIOs are increasingly directing AI into product development, engineering, and core intellectual property creation. AI is becoming a builder of products and capabilities, not just a tool for marketing and sales enablement. Budget signals reinforce this shift: Spending is shifting quickly toward systems of action, with workflow and orchestration platforms gaining share while infrastructure-centric providers lose momentum. The architecture of enterprise AI is evolving from a focus on compute toward a focus on capability. The takeaway for vendors is increasingly clear. The generic productivity pitch for AI is no longer sufficient. CIOs are now prioritizing AI that creates new products, modernizes legacy estates, and enables entirely new business models. Yes, efficiency gains still do matter, but they are no longer the strategic focus for enterprise AI in 2026. Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: With all the continued talk about the SaaS apocalypse and the challenges facing software in the age of AI and “vibe coding,” last week on the podcast 20VC, startup investor Gokul Rajaram shared his eight moats for sustainable software companies.

With all the continued talk about the SaaS apocalypse and the challenges facing software in the age of AI and “vibe coding,” last week on the podcast 20VC, startup investor Gokul Rajaram shared his eight moats for sustainable software companies. Similar to other frameworks around what makes a business durable, these are presented in the context of software and cloud. Let’s take a look at the eight moats for software: 1. Data Software companies with proprietary data have a clear advantage. This data can come from reaching a critical mass of customers or from exclusive agreements with third parties. Even in the age of vibe coding, where building software has less friction, some products are 10x better or only possible because of proprietary data. 2. Workflow Workflow refers to software that is mission-critical to running a business. Examples include accounting systems or e-commerce platforms. The more deeply embedded the software is in daily operations, the harder it becomes to replace. 3. Regulatory Companies in industries like financial services and healthcare often face heavy regulatory requirements. These include things like money transfer licenses or approvals to integrate with government systems. In some cases, only a limited number of players are allowed. In others, approval requires significant time and capital. 4. Distribution Getting software into the hands of customers is often expensive and time-consuming. Some companies build strong distribution advantages. For example, Apple’s App Store controls how billions of devices access software. Distribution is one of the hardest moats to build and one of the most durable once established. 5. Ecosystem Ecosystems emerge when a product becomes a platform and third-party developers build on top of it. Well-known examples include Salesforce.com and Shopify, each with thousands of integrations and add-ons. This moat takes significant time to develop and typically only happens once a platform becomes the clear leader in a large market. 6. Network Network effects are especially powerful in marketplace-driven software platforms that connect buyers and sellers. These often combine software functionality like ratings, reviews, and pricing with mechanisms to solve the chicken-and-egg problem. Over time, the value of the network compounds faster than user growth and eventually becomes the de facto standard. Continued below...

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: If your team uses GitHub and runs AI coding agents in CI, you need to read this right now.

If your team uses GitHub and runs AI coding agents in CI, you need to read this right now. We live in wild times! An autonomous AI bot called 'hackerbot-claw' — claiming to be powered by Claude Opus 4.5 has already compromised at least six major open source projects including repositories from Microsoft, Aqua Security, DataDog, and the Cloud Native Computing Foundation. It didn't stop there. StepSecurity researchers found it scanned nearly 47,391 GitHub repositories for misconfigured CI/CD workflows. The attack chain is surgical: - Scan for vulnerable workflows - Fork the target repository - Open a seemingly innocent pull request - Execute arbitrary code - Exfiltrate GitHub tokens No human. No phishing. No zero-day. Just a misconfigured GitHub Actions workflow and an AI agent that knew exactly what to look for. This is exactly why we built VXPwngard. https://lnkd.in/e_pxsHF4 We saw this attack chain coming from Red Team work we've done. When autonomous AI agents started executing it at machine speed across tens of thousands of repositories, we looked for a scanner that could catch it. We didn't find one. So we built it. VXPwngard is open source. It detects the exact pull_request_target + fork checkout kill chain that hackerbot-claw exploits, traces attacker-controlled inputs source-to-sink through your workflows, and is the first scanner to detect AI configuration injection attacks across CLAUDE.md, .mcp.json, and other AI agent config files. The bots are already scanning your repo. The question is whether you see them first. #CyberSecurity #Trivy #DevSecOps #AIAgents #SupplyChainSecurity #GitHubActions #OpenSource #Vigilant
