Write a LinkedIn post for sales about workflow. Angle: Sales Enablement has always evolved, but nothing has moved this fast.

Sales Enablement has always evolved, but nothing has moved this fast. I’ve seen this function transform more times than I can count. From binders and battlecards to LMS platforms and digital playbooks and then to CRM integration and revenue intelligence. Every wave, the best enablement professionals adapted. They didn’t abandon what worked, they built on it. This moment is no different, except the pace is. If I’m being honest, keeping up has required me to rethink some things I haven’t revisited in a while. Here’s what I believe Sales Enablement function and professionals need to be focused on right now: 1. Stop building content. Start curating intelligence. The problem was never a lack of assets. It was sellers not finding the right one at the right moment. Audit your content library with fresh eyes. What can an AI tool now surface automatically by deal stage, persona, or customer scenario? 2. Redefine what Sales Readiness means. A seller being ready used to mean they complete the required training. Now it means, can they work fluidly alongside AI tools in a live deal. Start introducing AI role-play and real-time coaching tools into your enablement programs. Not as a bonus feature but as a core competency. 3. Bring your best sellers into the AI conversation first. This is the mistake I see most often. New tools and sales programs get rolled out top-down, yet your more tenured sellers feel threatened, not empowered. Adoption stalls, leadership blames the technology or sales programs. Flip it. Make your most experienced sellers the champions and co-designers of your AI-assisted workflows. Their instincts (they need to feel that) + AI’s pattern recognition is your most powerful combination. 4. Measure differently. If you’re still evaluating sales performance with pre-AI benchmarks, you’re rewarding the wrong behaviors. Start tracking AI-assisted deal velocity, I am trying this and saw a significant improvement in the recent months. Monitor where human judgment changed an AI recommendation and whether it worked. Build your next Sales Enablement playbook with these learnings. Understanding your buyer, equipping your sales teams, connecting enablement to revenue outcomes - that’s still the job. Use AI to amplify your sales enablement efforts. Human × AI isn’t a technology story, it’s a people story. Sales Enablement has reinvented itself before. What’s happening right now is the biggest shift yet. I am curious, what’s the biggest shift you’ve had to make in your sales enablement approach this past year as this industry has never moved this fast. #SalesEnablement #RevenueEnablement #SalesLeadership #AIinSales #EnablementCommunity #SalesStrategy

Today, I am announcing the launch of AlphaBitCore. Our mission is to provide the operational AI fabric that product development teams are missing. As MCP adoption grows, connectivity gets easier, and coordination gets harder. That is where a control plane matters. What breaks first in real workflows: 1. Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: I've been toying with this notion for some time regarding how AI start-ups retain any sense of differentiation and, over time, "MOATs." With pre-training commoditized and scaling ubiquitous, early-stage companies face tough competition.

I've been toying with this notion for some time regarding how AI start-ups retain any sense of differentiation and, over time, "MOATs." With pre-training commoditized and scaling ubiquitous, early-stage companies face tough competition. Meanwhile, incumbents like Microsoft and Salesforce are moving like 20-person startups, leveraging decades of data, massive customer bases, and deep workflow knowledge. Startups, by contrast, are battling churn, "experimental ARR," and a crowded seed stage (10 competitors on average!). So, how do startups compete in the race to build agentic platforms 🤖—where software shifts from user-commanded to co-dependent? Signals and workflows are key, and incumbents have them in abundance. But here’s the potential play: buy the B2B agency. Agencies like MSSPs, marketing firms, and medical billing companies have spent years refining workflows and serving niche markets. These are treasure troves of data and insights—exactly what startups need to train models and build AI systems that compete with incumbents. Think of it as buying workflows and a treasure trove of consilient data for pre-training, not just building tech. We're starting to see this trend play out with companies like Landbase and Fyxer AI and several founders incubating ideas in adjacent segments like MSP/MSSP. Finally, I will put some thoughts on the hypothetical playbook and its rationale. Big thanks to Abhishek Dubey for the thoughtful edits and thoughts.

Write a LinkedIn post for devtools about workflow. Angle: AI coding tools are changing how software is built.

AI coding tools are changing how software is built. But using them effectively requires more engineering discipline, not less. Many developers are now “vibe coding” using AI assistants to generate code quickly and ship features faster. While this can significantly improve productivity, blindly accepting AI-generated code often leads to messy implementations, redundant logic, and codebases that become harder to maintain over time. Over time, while building software with AI coding tools, I started following a few practices that helped me move faster without losing engineering discipline. Things like: • planning the implementation with the AI before writing code • breaking work into phases instead of asking the AI to implement everything at once • reviewing every generated change carefully • testing each phase independently before moving forward • asking the AI for root cause analysis instead of immediately implementing fixes • avoiding pushing AI-generated code directly to Git without proper review These small practices make a big difference when working with AI-assisted development. I wrote a short article explaining the workflow I follow when vibe coding and how it helps keep development fast while maintaining code quality. Link in the comments. I’m still refining this workflow and would love to hear how others are using AI coding tools in their development process. #GenerativeAI #SoftwareEngineering #DeveloperProductivity #AICoding #DevTools

People have been asking more about my Claude Code setup. It started with Mike Murchison sharing his Chief of Staff framework. I kept building until it became a full AI Product Manager for my consulting practice. Here's what it does. You finish a customer discovery call. The transcript hits your inbox. Claude pulls it directly from Gmail and populates your entire engagement — stakeholder interview, product concept doc, requirements. If the client is already in the system, it updates their existing docs instead of starting from scratch. No copying. No pasting. No reformatting. One email. Every document ready. Then it builds the app. The system runs a 9-stage workflow: Discovery → Stakeholder Interview → Product Concept → Requirements → Vibe Code Prompt(auto-compiled from the previous two) → GTM → Launch → Review. When the concept and requirements are done, Claude compiles a build prompt automatically and hands it to Claude Code. Every document publishes to Confluence with one command — client has a full knowledge base before the next meeting. I ran it end-to-end on a fake client to test it. 60-minute discovery interview → transcript pulled from Gmail → concept doc and requirements populated automatically → app built in 20 minutes. Tenant portal, owner dashboard, contractor job view. All three interfaces functional. No team. No EA. No project managers. I can go from a discovery call to a working prototype faster than most teams can schedule a second meeting. Built with custom skills, 15 master templates, and MCP connections to Gmail, Google Calendar, Confluence, HubSpot and Jira. Thanks to Mike for the spark. This is what happens when you keep building on top of someone else's good idea. If you're a consultant, PM, or solo operator — happy to share how it works. Write a LinkedIn post for ai about workflow. Angle: AI is no longer a luxury for the tech-savvy; it has become the underlying substrate for global finance, energy management, healthcare, and national security.

AI is no longer a luxury for the tech-savvy; it has become the underlying substrate for global finance, energy management, healthcare, and national security. This shift from "software product" to "infrastructure" carries profound implications for how we build, regulate, and power our world. 1. The Anatomy of AI Infrastructure To understand AI as infrastructure, we must look beyond the chatbot interface. The true "stack" is a massive physical and logical undertaking. The Physical Layer: GPU Clusters and "AI Factories" The data centers of the 2010s were libraries; the data centers of the 2020s are factories. Modern AI-focused data centers operate at power densities unimaginable a decade ago. Specialized Compute: General-purpose CPUs have been sidelined by massive clusters of GPUs and specialized ASICs (Application-Specific Integrated Circuits) like Google’s TPUs. Cooling Systems: Traditional air cooling is being replaced by liquid cooling to handle the thermal output of high-density racks, which now consume upwards of 100kW per rack. Geographic Shifts: Data center siting is no longer just about proximity to fiber optics; it is about proximity to stable, high-capacity electrical grids. Regions with surplus energy, like the Nordic countries or parts of the American Midwest, have become the "ports" of the AI era. The Logical Layer: Foundation Models Just as the internet relies on TCP/IP, the AI-driven economy relies on a handful of Foundation Models. These models act as the "base layer" upon which thousands of niche applications are built. When a foundational model updates or experiences downtime, entire industries—from automated legal review to real-time supply chain optimization—feel the ripple effects. 2. The Economic Shift: From Tool to General Purpose Technology (GPT) Economists classify certain innovations as General Purpose Technologies (GPTs). To qualify, a technology must be pervasive, improve rapidly, and spawn further innovation. AI has met all three criteria. The Productivity Paradox and the Infrastructure Lag We are currently in the "installation phase." Historically, when a new infrastructure arrives (like electricity), productivity growth initially slows down because companies must reorganize their entire workflow to use it. By 2026, we are finally seeing the "deployment phase" where AI-native infrastructure—not just "AI-added" software—is driving measurable macroeconomic gains.

Angle: Some thoughts on agentic AI, climate science, and verification: I've increasingly been experimenting with agentic AI tools to do climate science, which has been both mind-blowing (when things go right the first time!) and frustrating (when a seemingly simple ask is not done correctly).

Some thoughts on agentic AI, climate science, and verification: I've increasingly been experimenting with agentic AI tools to do climate science, which has been both mind-blowing (when things go right the first time!) and frustrating (when a seemingly simple ask is not done correctly). I am currently moving from "just playing" to trying to formalize a new workflow using these tools. In addition to the technical side (which I may post about later), it forces an interesting mental shift towards correctly formulating the problem to begin with, and the all-important #verification. My emerging approach to verification includes: - the "sniff test", e.g. does this make sense given our knowledge of the climate system? - asking the agent to explain and quote key parts of its code - asking the agent to write tests for all functions used Climate data are messy (spatiotemporal, big, correlated, nonstationary) and there is no option to absolutely verify a result, unlike e.g. Lean in math. How are you using these tools? How are you verifying?

Write a LinkedIn post for ai about workflow. Angle: A few thoughts on AI automation… Whenever we automate a task, we actually solve two problems.

A few thoughts on AI automation… Whenever we automate a task, we actually solve two problems. The first problem is the obvious one that the task itself was solving. The second problem is the new challenge we can now address, given that we’ve freed up our time. The good thing is that there’s an abundance of problems to work on and value to add. The bad thing is that it’s often not obvious which second problem to tackle next. It’s scary when you don’t know what the next problem could be, but exciting when you have a good idea and just haven’t been able to get to it yet. So, whenever you automate a task or workflow, spend time exploring how you and others can benefit from this automation in moving from less interesting to more interesting problems at work. This takes imagination, especially as technology not only replaces tasks but enables us to tackle new tasks we couldn't previously accomplish. If we ignore what the second problem could be, it’s easy to waste any saved time from automation on low-value activities. #AI #Automation #FutureOfWork

While developing it, I realized that financial intelligence becomes more powerful when independent analytical components run in parallel and their insights are combined into a final prediction. So the system evolved into a modular multi-agent architecture, where multiple specialized agents analyze different signals simultaneously. Current agents in the system: • Quant Agent – Generates Bear (P05), Base (P50), and Bull (P95) price scenarios using quantile regression. • NLP Agent – Analyzes earnings call transcripts using Llama 3 via Groq, with Gemini API as a fallback, to capture management sentiment and tone. • News Agent – Performs sentiment analysis on financial news headlines with point-in-time filtering to avoid look-ahead bias. • Competitor Agent – Benchmarks companies against industry peers to provide relative performance insights. System orchestration: The system runs asynchronously using FastAPI, allowing all agents to execute in parallel and improving response speed. Data sources integrated: Earlier versions of the system relied on yFinance for historical data, but the pipeline was later upgraded to use FRED, Finnhub, and NewsAPI to incorporate real macroeconomic, market, and news signals. • FRED API – Macroeconomic indicators • Finnhub API – Financial market data • NewsAPI – Real-time financial news signals Explainability - Outputs from all agents are aggregated through an ensemble layer, and predictions are made explainable using SHAP feature attribution. Current system performance • Parallel agents per request: 4 • Quant model inference latency: ~0.2 seconds • Full AI analysis time: ~8–15 seconds • Feature vector size: 21 financial indicators Sharing the initial architecture diagram here. Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: Perplexity Computer, just announced, is Perplexity's answer to Anthropic's Claude CoWork, and OpenClaw.

Perplexity Computer, just announced, is Perplexity's answer to Anthropic's Claude CoWork, and OpenClaw. OpenClaw is an open-source project whose creator was recently hired by OpenAI. It's more relevant to compare Perplexity Computer to Claude CoWork. Anthropic Models vs "Anyone's" Models ========================== Perplexity Computer actually uses an Anthropic model, Opus 4.6 as its "core reasoning engine". In that respect it is not "anti Anthropic". In this context, Perplexity describes "core reasoning" as "the orchestrator and primary decision-maker for the overall workflow". (It does not mean that all Chain-Of-Thought processing is delegated only to Opus 4.6 - see below). Perplexity notes that Perplexity Computer is built to orchestrate many different LLMs from many different vendors, so it might route some work to ChatGPT 5.2 or Gemini 3. This is different from Anthropic's Claude CoWork, which uses Anthropic LLMs only, not LLMs from Google or OpenAI or anyone else. Tool Use (which impacts what tasks they can do for you) ==================================== Both Perplexity Computer and Claude CoWork can call a wide variety of tools, often exposed via their MCP servers, to do things like update your CRM (careful!), make appointments on your calendar or edit your spreadsheets and slides. Local vs Cloud ========= Even though CoWork uses an LLM running on an Anthropic server, key parts of its work are executed locally on your machine. Perplexity Computer runs completely on a server operated by Perplexity. That makes CoWork able to perform direct, local edits on your machine in a way that Perplexity Computer can't (yet). Perplexity Computer however can perform a string of related tasks even when your computer is off. Claude CoWork can't. It needs your computer to be on.

Whether you're building for a boutique insurance brokerage, a robotics manufacturing floor, or the consumer audio industry: Those architecture decisions get a lot easier once you know how to communicate the problem first. Sit with people. Hear it in their words. Turn it into something you can actually build. Then flip it - strip out the technical stuff and give them decisions in the language they speak. The end-user doesn’t care about your architecture. They care about what it lets them do. Build for that. Thanks to Eli Newman and Eli Cohen for setting this up.

Write a LinkedIn post for sales about workflow. Angle: AI products are breaking retention benchmarks.

AI products are breaking retention benchmarks. This a16z chart shows why👇 The average retention pattern in consumer SaaS looked something like this: Week-1: steep retention drop Month-1: retention keeps falling Month-6: companies are fighting to keep churn stable That’s the benchmark most companies use to build against. LLMs are not following that pattern. Look at ChatGPT’s retention pattern: - Rises from month 2 - Steadily rises till month 23 - And then a sharp rise post month 23 For Gemini, the pattern is different, but retention is still high. Retention dips early, but reverses around month 10 and keeps rising. At this scale, this retention curve is almost unheard of. But why? Because LLMs are habit-forming products. Here's what the average AI user journey looks like: Users start with prompts, then move to drafts, then to coding, analysis, and research, and finally to internal workflows. This drives: • Higher session frequency • Stronger switching costs • Broader surface area Eventually, AI starts seeping into every task, from writing to coding. And now AI is integrated into almost all the tech products we use today. This changes the economics where higher long-term retention: > lowers CAC > increases LTV > reduces switching costs At this scale, the old SaaS playbook stops working. You’re looking at a new wave of companies where the old rules of retention do not apply. ---------------------------- Today, I run these private communities that might help you: → SaasRise: for founders scaling from $1M to $100M → GrowthRise: for B2B Sales and Marketing leaders → Pulse: Reinventing the Daily Newspaper in the Age of AI (link in comments)

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: Agentic workflows are cool, But.....

Agentic workflows are cool, But..... Being chained to your computer to run them? No thanks. I needed my AI agents to actually be autonomous. Here's the problem: manually triggering these workflows locally is a drag. Great for testing. But not a long-term solution. Who wants to be on call at 3 AM to kick off a process? ✔️ The dream is 24/7, scalable autonomy. That's where cloud deployment comes in. Suddenly, webhooks, cron jobs, and agent-to-agent triggers are on the table. → Truly independent, responsive AI systems are possible. I've been experimenting with Modal (serverless platform). And it's a game changer. Here's how it works: • You deploy your workflows as webhooks. • Modal handles the scaling (server spin-up/spin-down). • And it's surprisingly cost-effective. For example, I set up a daily news summarization agent. Modal triggers it at a specific time each day. I even have another agent that triggers the summarizer based on specific events. Think of the possibilities... If you're building agentic workflows, give Modal a shot. Start with one workflow. Scale from there. Stop babysitting your agents; let them run themselves. What's the most annoying manual task you're automating?
